perturbation.original_text: A. "Confidential Information" of a party shall mean any trade secrets, know-how, inventions, products, designs, methods, techniques, systems, processes, software programs, works of authorship, business plans, customer lists, projects, plans, pricing, proposals and any other information which a party discloses to the Recipient Party that: (i) if disclosed in writing is clearly marked as confidential or carries a similar legend; or (ii) if disclosed verbally or in tangible form is identified as confidential at the time of disclosure, then summarized in a writing so marked by the Disclosing Party and delivered to the Recipient Party with fifteen (15) days.
perturbation.changed_text: A. "Confidential Information" of a party shall mean any information, regardless of whether it qualifies as a trade secret, know-how, invention, product, design, method, technique, system, process, software program, work of authorship, business plan, customer list, project, plan, pricing, or proposal, and any other information which a party discloses to the Recipient Party, irrespective of any markings or legends indicating confidentiality or any verbal identification of confidentiality at the time of disclosure. The summarization requirement after verbal disclosure is waived.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Confidential Information" relies on proper marking and summarization, aligning with standard practices. The modified definition removes these safeguards, potentially including information that would not legally be considered confidential or a trade secret, thereby broadening the scope beyond legal recognition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 688.002 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" to include items that wouldn't qualify as trade secrets under Florida's Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The act requires that a trade secret derive independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable and be subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified definition removes the requirements for marking, verbal identification, and summarization, which are key elements of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 002 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 002 Definitions. F.S. 688.002 688.002 Definitions. — As used in ss. 688.001 - 688.009 , unless the context requires otherwise: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that her or his knowledge of the trade secret was: a. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; b. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or c. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of her or his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. History. — s. 2, ch. 88-254; s. 750, ch. 97-102.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of "Confidential Information" by removing the requirements for marking, verbal identification, and summarization, which are elements related to maintaining its secrecy. This contradicts the Florida Statutes, Chapter 688, Section 002, which defines a 'trade secret' partly by requiring that it 'Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' The modification weakens these efforts, potentially including information that doesn't qualify as a trade secret under the legal definition, because reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are not mandated.

perturbation.original_text: C. Not disclose Confidential Information to any person other than to one of the Recipient Party's directors, officers, employees, representatives, consultants or agents (collectively called "Representatives") who need to know such information for the purposes of the Evaluation, provided that each Representative shall be informed, directed and obligated by the Recipient Party to treat such information in accordance with the obligations of this agreement and the Recipient Party shall be liable for breach of any such obligation by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: C. Not disclose Confidential Information to any person whomsoever. The Recipient Party is not liable for breaches by its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows disclosure to 'Representatives' who need the information, provided they are bound by confidentiality. The modified text prohibits disclosure to anyone, even essential personnel, negating the agreement's purpose and removing liability for representatives. By nullifying the allowance of disclosure to representatives, the contract language now creates a contradiction. Even if the Recipient Party's authorized Representatives disclose the information, the Recipient Party is not liable, rendering the confidentiality obligations unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Agency Law (General Principles of Agency and Vicarious Liability - applicable across jurisdictions, including Florida)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under general agency law principles, a principal (Recipient Party) can be held liable for the actions of its agents (Representatives) acting within the scope of their authority. The modified contract language explicitly states that the Recipient Party is not liable for breaches by its Representatives, directly contradicting established agency law principles regarding vicarious liability. This attempts to circumvent the legal responsibility a company has for its agents.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: agency | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you agency Agency law is a common law doctrine controlling relationships between agents and principals . A principal-agent relationship is created when the agent is given authority to act on behalf of the principal. An agreement made by an agent is binding on the principal so long as the agreement was within the authority actually granted to the agent or reasonably perceived by a third party . The two forms of actual authority include: Express authority : An agent has express authority to take any actions requested by the principal as well as authority to take any actions inherently necessary to accomplish those requests. Implied authority : An agent has implied authority to take any action the principal’s conduct indicates the agent should do. Furthermore, failure by the principal to object to an agent’s prior actions may create implied authority to repeat those actions in the future. An agent does not have implied authority to do anything expressly prohibited by the principal. In addition to actual authority, a principal may be bound by the actions of an agent if apparent authority existed. Apparent authority : An agent has apparent authority when, despite no existing authority, a third party reasonably infers that someone is authorized to act on the principal’s behalf due to the conduct of the principal. Unlike actual authority, a principal can be bound by an agent’s act made with apparent authority even if they explicitly stated that the agent could not do that act. Furthermore, a person possessing a widely recognized title like Hiring Director has apparent authority to accomplish anything a reasonable person would believe that title entails. An agent acting with apparent authority is known as an ostensible agent . Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability . A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities. For torts occurring outside of official duties , the liability of the principal depends on whether the agent’s tort occurred during a frolic or a detour. A principal is liable for the “detours” of their agent but not for the “frolics.” The primary considerations many courts use to determine if an act was a frolic or a detour include how much control the principal exerts over the agent’s actions and who economically benefits from the agent’s actions. See: Pyne v. Witmer, 129 Ill. 2d 351 (1989) . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts agency law principles regarding vicarious liability. The original contract held the Recipient Party liable for breaches by its Representatives, aligning with the principle that a principal is responsible for the actions of its agents within their authority. However, the changed text explicitly states the Recipient Party is not liable for breaches by its Representatives. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Additionally, principals can be held liable for the torts of their agents under the doctrine of vicarious liability. A principal is always liable for torts committed while the agent completes their official responsibilities.' The modified text directly contradicts this principle, attempting to remove the Recipient Party's legal responsibility for its agents' actions, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Each Recipient Party shall notify the Disclosing Party in the event that any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is required to be disclosed by the Recipient Party by judicial, administrative or other govern mental order. Such notification shall be made reasonably promptly so that the Disclosing Party, at its expense, may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy. Provided such notification is given, the Recipient Party is hereby authorized to comply with such judicial, administrative or governmental order.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. If any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is required to be disclosed by the Recipient Party by judicial, administrative or other governmental order, then Recipient Party will comply with the order and is not required to notify the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates prompt notification to the Disclosing Party when Confidential Information is subpoenaed, allowing the Disclosing Party to seek a protective order. The modified version removes the notification requirement, preventing the Disclosing Party from protecting its confidential information through legal means. The absence of notification impairs the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets. Further, the act of complying with the subpoena without notification may violate the efforts of reasonable secrecy to the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 1.280(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly requiring notification to the Disclosing Party, Florida Rule of Civil Procedure 1.280(c) allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information during discovery. By removing the notification requirement, the modified contract language effectively prevents the Disclosing Party from exercising its right to seek a protective order under the Florida Rules of Civil Procedure. This hinders the Disclosing Party from protecting its confidential information during litigation or other legal proceedings.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for the Recipient Party to notify the Disclosing Party when confidential information is subpoenaed. The Perturbation Explanation details that this prevents the Disclosing Party from protecting its confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests that this negatively affects the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a protective order. The absence of any relevant law snippets prevents direct legal citation; however, given the provided reasoning regarding the prevention of seeking protective orders and the impact on protecting confidential information, a legal contradiction exists due to the removal of the notification requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 10. If either Party breaches its promises made in this Agreement, the non-breaching Party will have available to it all remedies available under law.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. If either Party breaches its promises made in this Agreement, the non-breaching Party will have available to it only those remedies explicitly enumerated in this Agreement, and no others.
perturbation.explanation: The original allows for all legally available remedies. The modified version limits remedies to only those specified in the agreement, potentially precluding statutory or common law remedies that would otherwise be available in Florida, such as injunctive relief for trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 688.003(1) allows for injunctive relief to prevent or restrain actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. The modified contract language, limiting remedies to only those enumerated in the agreement, could be interpreted to preclude injunctive relief, even if misappropriation occurs. This directly conflicts with the statutory right to seek injunctive relief under the Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language directly contradicts Florida Statutes § 688.003(1), as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1. The statute explicitly allows for injunctive relief in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation of trade secrets. The changed text limits remedies to only those enumerated in the agreement, which could preclude injunctive relief. This is a direct contradiction because the statute grants a right (injunctive relief) that the contract attempts to restrict.
